items.q: What is the difference between a megacity and a world city?
items.correct: A megacity is defined by population (over 10 million); a world city is defined by global economic and cultural influence (e.g. New York, London) — so a city can be one without the other.
items.misconception: Megacities and world cities are the same thing.
items.random_wrong: A megacity has over 1 million people and a world city has over 5 million.
items.why: Dhaka and Kinshasa are megacities but not world cities; Zurich is a world city but not a megacity. The distinction is quantitative (size) vs qualitative (function and global connections).

items.q: What does Frank's dependency theory argue?
items.correct: That LICs (periphery) are kept poor by exploitative trade relationships with HICs (core) — underdevelopment is actively produced by capitalism, not a stage that countries grow out of.
items.misconception: Dependency theory says LICs are poor because they depend too much on foreign aid.
items.random_wrong: Dependency theory is the same as Rostow's modernisation theory.
items.why: Dependency theory is structural and critical, opposing Rostow. The 'dependency' is on the global economic system, not aid. Students confuse the word 'dependency' with aid reliance, missing the political-economic critique.

items.q: Why do TNCs locate production in LICs?
items.correct: Lower labour costs, weaker regulation, access to raw materials, large potential markets, and government incentives like tax breaks in special economic zones.
items.misconception: Because TNCs want to develop poorer countries and lift them out of poverty.
items.random_wrong: Because LICs have better infrastructure than HICs.
items.why: Students attribute moral motives. TNCs are profit-driven; any development benefit is a side effect, not a goal — a critical distinction for evaluation questions.
Correct answer: Immanuel Wallerstein. “George Modelski” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Which zone in Wallerstein's model supplies raw materials?
items.why: Correct answer: Periphery. “Core” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Approximately how many years does a Modelski hegemonic cycle last?
items.why: The real figure is 100. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
